serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Kadai Mutton is approximately 250 g, comprising several pieces of bone-in or boneless mutton with sauce, served alongside rice or Indian bread.

nutrient_source.title: Kadai Mutton (Spicy) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Kadai Mutton (Spicy), confidence 0.95. All per_100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated at 4.5 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb/mutton (USDA NDB 17174) as the closest approved secondary source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Meat and Vegetable Dishes — Analogous Estimate Based on Low-GI Protein-Dominant Curry
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Kadai Mutton in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous protein-dominant meat curry entries in the Sydney GI Database. The dish has low carbohydrate content (7.44 g/100g), high protein (14.05 g/100g), and moderate fat (11.58 g/100g), all of which attenuate postprandial glucose response. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 18.6) / 100 ≈ 6.5 for a 250 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg/100g threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g per meal, request reduced-salt preparation, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or papadums to keep total sodium intake within the 2000 mg/day target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (7.44 g/100g) and high protein (14.05 g/100g) support glycaemic modulation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kadai Mutton is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in a 200–250 g portion without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan; the low carbohydrate load and high protein content support stable postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.5 g/100g estimated) and cholesterol (85 mg/100g) from red mutton meat present cardiovascular risk at regular consumption
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Kadai Mutton to occasional consumption (once per week or less), request preparation with minimal added oil, and pair with high-fibre vegetables to partially offset the saturated fat and cholesterol burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton is high in arachidonic acid; nightshade ingredients (tomato, chilli, bell pepper) may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise red meat frequency, monitor individual joint response to nightshade vegetables present in this dish, and consider requesting olive oil substitution to reduce the pro-inflammatory fat profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kadai Mutton (Spicy) is a popular North Indian and Pakistani dish widely served in Singapore's Indian restaurants and hawker stalls. It consists of tender pieces of mutton (goat or sheep meat) stir-fried in a traditional iron wok (kadai or karahi) with tomatoes, onions, bell peppers, green chillies, garlic, ginger, and a blend of aromatic Indian spices including cumin, coriander, garam masala, and red chilli powder. The cooking method involves high-heat wok frying that produces a semi-dry, intensely flavoured sauce with a characteristic smoky edge. The texture is tender and slightly chewy with a rich, spiced tomato-based coating. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 192 kcal, 14.05 g protein, 11.58 g fat, 7.44 g carbohydrate, 4.69 g sugar, 0.3 g dietary fibre, and 467 mg sodium. The high sodium content makes it a concern for hypertension management, while the low carbohydrate and high protein profile make it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes when paired with low-GI accompaniments. The saturated fat and cholesterol from red mutton meat require moderation for cardiovascular health. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal. It is commonly served at lunch and dinner as a main course alongside rice, roti, or naan in Singapore's Indian cuisine context.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked mutton entry as the closest approved secondary source. GI estimated from analogous protein-dominant curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific Kadai Mutton (Spicy) preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry, and no sufficiently close match was found in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for the composite dish as prepared. Saturated fat estimated at 4.5 g/100g from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked lamb/mutton (NDB 17174) as the closest approved secondary source; this is an approximation and should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on protein-dominant meat curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Kadai Mutton was found. Halal status assumed based on mutton being a halal-permissible meat when slaughtered according to Islamic rites; preparation context in Singapore Indian restaurants typically follows halal standards but individual outlet certification should be verified.
